title: Why Nvidia Refuses to Become a Cloud: The 'As Little As Possible' Doctrine

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
Nvidia generates more cash than most cloud providers and sits at the center of the AI infrastructure stack. It backstops CoreWeave to the tune of billions. It has invested in multiple neoclouds. The obvious question is: why doesn't Nvidia just become a hyperscaler itself? Huang's answer reveals a corporate philosophy that is both disciplined and counterintuitive: "Do as much as needed, as little as possible."

The doctrine works like a filter. For any capability, Huang asks whether Nvidia's involvement is irreplaceable. CUDA, NVLink, domain-specific libraries, the co-design of hardware and algorithms—these are things that, in Huang's estimation, would simply not exist if Nvidia didn't build them. He points to cuLitho, a computational lithography library, as a case where "if we didn't create it, nobody would have." These pass the filter. Cloud infrastructure does not. "The world has lots of clouds. If I didn't do it, somebody would show up."

This philosophy extends to Nvidia's financial support of the neocloud ecosystem. Huang is explicit that Nvidia doesn't want to be in the financing business. The company invests in CoreWeave, Nscale, and Nebius not to capture cloud economics but to ensure that the Nvidia platform reaches as many industries, countries, and use cases as possible. The goal is maximum ecosystem breadth, not vertical integration. Once these companies establish their own flywheels, Nvidia steps back.

The same logic governs Nvidia's investments in frontier labs. The $30 billion into OpenAI and the investment in Anthropic are framed as ecosystem necessities, not strategic acquisitions. Huang supports these companies because "the world needs them to exist" and because their scale requires backing that traditional VCs cannot provide. But the moment the need is met, the doctrine kicks back in: do as little as possible.

There's an implicit bet embedded in this philosophy: that the platform layer is more durable and more valuable than the infrastructure or application layers. By refusing to compete with its own customers, Nvidia preserves the neutrality that makes every cloud, every lab, and every enterprise willing to build on its stack. The restraint is the moat.
